prompt: What is the fundamental unit of work in Datadog APM distributed tracing?
explanation: A span represents a single unit of work or operation. Spans are the building blocks of traces. Each span has a name, start time, duration, and optional key-value tags.

prompt: A trace in Datadog APM represents which of the following?

choices.text: The complete end-to-end path of a request as it traverses multiple services
A single HTTP request to one service

explanation: A trace represents the entire journey of a request as it propagates through a distributed system, potentially spanning multiple services, databases, and external calls. It is composed of one or more spans.

prompt: In Datadog APM, the root span of a trace has no parent span ID.
explanation: The root span is the first span in a trace and therefore has no parent. All other spans in the trace are descendants of the root span, forming a tree structure.

prompt: Which Datadog library is used to instrument a Node.js application for APM tracing?
explanation: The dd-trace library is the official Datadog tracing client for Node.js. Similar language-specific libraries exist for Python (ddtrace), Java (dd-trace-java), Go (dd-trace-go), and others.

prompt: What does the 'p99 latency' metric represent in Datadog APM?

choices.text: The average latency across all requests
The latency value below which 99% of requests fall
The maximum latency observed in the past 99 minutes
The latency of the 99th request in a trace

explanation: p99 latency is the 99th percentile latency. It means 99% of all requests complete faster than this value. It is useful for understanding tail latency and the worst-case experience for most users.

prompt: In the context of APM RED metrics, what does the 'E' stand for?
explanation: RED stands for Rate (requests per second), Errors (number of failed requests), and Duration (distribution of request latencies). These three metrics are the foundation for monitoring service health in Datadog APM.

prompt: Datadog APM auto-instrumentation can trace supported frameworks without any code changes to the application.
explanation: Datadog APM supports auto-instrumentation for many popular frameworks and libraries. For example, Java applications can be auto-instrumented by attaching the dd-java-agent.jar at startup, which automatically instruments supported libraries without code changes.

prompt: What is the primary purpose of the Datadog Service Map?

choices.text: To display the geographic location of your servers
To visualize service dependencies and the health of connections between services
To map hostnames to IP addresses
To show the file structure of your deployed application

explanation: The Service Map provides a real-time visualization of your service architecture, showing how services depend on each other and color-coding each service and connection based on its current health (monitor status).

prompt: Which identifier uniquely links all spans belonging to the same trace?
explanation: The trace ID is a globally unique identifier that is propagated through all services involved in handling a request. Every span generated by that request carries the same trace ID, allowing Datadog to reconstruct the full trace.

prompt: What is the term for a span that is called by another span, establishing a hierarchical relationship?
explanation: A child span is a span that is created within the context of another (parent) span. The parent-child relationship is established via the parent span ID stored in the child span, forming a tree hierarchy within a trace.

prompt: In Datadog APM, what is a 'resource name'?

choices.text: The hostname of the server handling the request
The specific query or endpoint being traced, such as a URL path or SQL query
The cloud resource ID assigned by AWS or GCP
The name of the Datadog Agent collecting the trace

explanation: The resource name provides a more specific description of the work being done within a service. For a web server, it might be the URL route; for a database span, it might be the normalized SQL query. It scopes metrics within a service.

prompt: Head-based sampling makes the keep/drop decision at the beginning of the trace, before any spans have been collected.
explanation: Head-based sampling decides whether to keep or drop a trace at the very start (the head) of the request. This decision is propagated via context to all downstream services. It is efficient but cannot prioritize traces based on outcomes like errors.

prompt: What is the key advantage of tail-based sampling over head-based sampling?

choices.text: It uses less memory on the agent
It can make keep/drop decisions based on the full context of a completed trace, such as preserving all error traces

explanation: Tail-based sampling buffers spans until a trace is complete, then applies sampling rules based on the full context, for example, keeping all traces that contain an error. This is more intelligent than head-based sampling but requires more buffering resources.

prompt: Which Datadog APM feature allows you to search and filter traces using tag-based queries?
explanation: Trace Search and Analytics (formerly App Analytics) lets you search and filter indexed spans using tag-based queries, making it possible to find specific traces or analyze aggregate patterns across millions of requests.

prompt: Which of the following are part of the RED metrics framework used in Datadog APM? (Select all that apply)
explanation: RED stands for Rate, Errors, and Duration. These three signal types, throughput, error rate, and latency, are the primary metrics for assessing the health of a service in Datadog APM. Disk I/O is an infrastructure metric, not part of RED.

prompt: What does the Datadog Continuous Profiler primarily measure?

choices.text: Code-level performance metrics such as CPU usage, memory allocation, and wall time by function
HTTP response codes returned by a service

explanation: The Continuous Profiler captures code-level performance data continuously in production, showing which functions and lines of code consume the most CPU time, allocate the most memory, or block on locks, enabling engineers to pinpoint bottlenecks.

prompt: The Datadog Continuous Profiler requires stopping and restarting your application to collect profiling data.
explanation: The Continuous Profiler is designed to run continuously in production without requiring restarts. It uses low-overhead sampling techniques to collect profiling data at regular intervals without significantly impacting application performance.

prompt: How does Datadog APM correlate traces with logs?

choices.text: By matching timestamps within a 1-second window
By injecting the trace ID and span ID into log entries
By using the service name as a join key
By scanning log messages for error keywords

explanation: Datadog correlates traces and logs by injecting trace_id and span_id into log records at the time of logging. This allows you to pivot from a specific span in a trace directly to the corresponding log lines generated during that span's execution.

prompt: In Datadog APM, what is the 'service' concept?

choices.text: A set of processes that do the same job, grouped together for metric aggregation
A single running container or virtual machine
An individual span within a trace

explanation: In Datadog APM, a service is a grouping of processes or endpoints that perform the same job (e.g., a web server, a database client, or a microservice). All spans from processes sharing the same service name are aggregated together.

prompt: Which of the following profiling types does the Datadog Continuous Profiler support? (Select all that apply)
explanation: The Datadog Continuous Profiler supports CPU profiling (identifying hot code paths), memory/heap allocation profiling (finding memory-hungry code), and lock/mutex contention profiling (finding blocking synchronization). Network bandwidth is monitored via Network Performance Monitoring, not the Continuous Profiler.

prompt: What is the default sampling rate applied by the Datadog Agent when no explicit sampling rules are configured?

choices.text: The agent uses adaptive (automatic) sampling to target approximately 5 traces per second per service
0%, no traces are kept without explicit rules

explanation: By default, the Datadog Agent applies Adaptive Sampling, which aims to keep approximately 5 traces per second per service/environment combination. This rate is automatically adjusted based on traffic volume to maintain representative data without overwhelming storage.

prompt: Span tags in Datadog APM are key-value pairs that provide additional context about the operation a span represents.
explanation: Span tags are key-value pairs attached to spans that provide additional metadata and context. Examples include http.status_code, db.type, and custom business tags. Tags are searchable and can be used in trace queries and analytics.

prompt: Which tag is automatically added by Datadog to indicate the HTTP response status code of a web request span?
explanation: Datadog uses the OpenTelemetry-aligned semantic convention http.status_code to tag web request spans with their HTTP response status. This is one of many standard tags automatically set by Datadog's tracing libraries.

prompt: In Datadog APM, what is an 'entry span' (also called a 'top-level span')?

choices.text: The first span ever collected from a service
The first span created by a service for a given trace, which is the entry point into that service
A span that has no child spans
A span with a duration of zero

explanation: An entry span (top-level span) is the first span within a service for a given trace request. It represents the entry point into that service and is the span used to calculate service-level metrics like request rate and latency.

prompt: What is the purpose of Datadog's APM Error Tracking feature?

choices.text: To track the number of 5xx HTTP responses from load balancers
To automatically group similar errors from traces and logs into issues for easier triage
To send error alerts via PagerDuty
To replay failed transactions in a staging environment

explanation: APM Error Tracking automatically groups similar errors into issues based on their fingerprint (error type, message, and stack trace). This de-duplicates noisy error streams and helps engineers focus on the highest-impact issues rather than individual occurrences.

prompt: When correlating APM traces with Datadog Logs, which fields need to be present in log records? (Select all that apply)
explanation: At a minimum, dd.trace_id and dd.span_id must be injected into log records for APM-to-log correlation to work. Datadog's tracing libraries can automatically inject these fields. dd.service and dd.env are useful for filtering but are not required for the correlation link itself.

prompt: What does the 'throughput' metric represent in Datadog APM?

choices.text: The number of bytes transferred per second
The number of requests processed per unit of time
The CPU utilization of the service
The number of open database connections

explanation: Throughput in APM measures the rate of requests a service is processing, typically expressed as requests per second (rps) or requests per minute (rpm). It corresponds to the 'R' (Rate) in the RED metrics framework.

prompt: Datadog APM can automatically detect and display the service dependencies shown in the Service Map without any manual configuration.
explanation: The Service Map is automatically populated by analyzing the parent-child span relationships in collected traces. As long as services are instrumented and traces are flowing, Datadog will automatically discover and display service-to-service dependencies.

prompt: Which environment variable is used to set the service name when using Datadog's dd-trace library?
explanation: DD_SERVICE is the standard environment variable used to configure the service name for Datadog tracing libraries. It is part of the Unified Service Tagging approach alongside DD_ENV and DD_VERSION.

prompt: In Datadog APM, what are 'Indexed Spans' (formerly Analyzed Spans)?

choices.text: Spans that have been stored in a long-term cold storage archive
Spans that are fully stored and individually searchable in Trace Search and Analytics
Spans that have been exported to a third-party system
Spans associated with a monitor alert

explanation: Indexed Spans are spans that have been individually indexed and made fully searchable in Trace Search and Analytics. They are retained for 15 days by default. The number of indexed spans you store determines part of your APM billing.

prompt: What does p50 latency represent?

choices.text: The median latency, 50% of requests complete faster than this value
The average of the top 50 slowest requests
The latency goal that must be met 50% of the time

explanation: p50 (50th percentile) latency is the median. Half of all requests complete faster than this value. It represents a typical user experience, in contrast to p95 or p99, which represent the experience for users at the tail of the latency distribution.

prompt: In Datadog APM, a span can have at most one parent span.
explanation: In standard distributed tracing (including Datadog APM and OpenTelemetry), a span can have at most one parent span. Traces form a tree structure, not a DAG (directed acyclic graph). Each span stores exactly one parent span ID (or none, if it is the root span).

prompt: Which component in the Datadog platform is responsible for receiving spans from application tracing libraries and forwarding them to the Datadog backend?
explanation: The Datadog Agent runs alongside your application and acts as a local aggregation and forwarding layer. Tracing libraries send spans to the Agent (by default on port 8126 via HTTP), which then batches and forwards them to the Datadog backend.

prompt: Which of the following can be used to set sampling rules in Datadog APM? (Select all that apply)

choices.text: Sampling rules configured in the Datadog Agent's datadog.yaml
Remote Configuration in the Datadog UI

explanation: APM sampling rules can be configured via the DD_TRACE_SAMPLE_RATE environment variable (tracer-level), the Agent's datadog.yaml configuration, and via Remote Configuration which allows updating sampling rules from the Datadog UI without redeploying. DD_LOGS_INJECTION is for log correlation, not sampling.

prompt: Which flame graph color scheme does the Datadog Continuous Profiler use to indicate the highest CPU usage?

choices.text: Blue frames indicate the hottest code paths
Wider/larger frames indicate functions that consume more of the profiled resource
Red frames indicate the hottest code paths

explanation: In flame graphs (used by Datadog's Continuous Profiler), the width of a frame is proportional to the amount of time (or resource) that function occupies in the total profile. Wider frames indicate higher resource consumption. The x-axis position is not meaningful for ordering, only the relative width matters.

prompt: What is 'Trace Retention' in Datadog APM?

choices.text: The process of sampling which traces to send to Datadog
The filters that control which indexed spans are kept in long-term storage for search and analytics
The automatic deletion of traces older than 30 days
A feature that keeps a copy of all traces in an S3 bucket

explanation: Trace Retention Filters are configurable rules that determine which indexed spans to keep in Datadog's search and analytics storage. Datadog provides intelligent default retention filters that capture error traces, high-latency traces, and a sample of all traffic.

prompt: Datadog APM supports OpenTelemetry (OTel) instrumentation, allowing traces to be ingested from OTel SDKs.
explanation: Datadog fully supports OpenTelemetry instrumentation. You can send OTel traces to the Datadog Agent via OTLP (OpenTelemetry Protocol), or use the Datadog Exporter for the OTel Collector. This gives teams the flexibility to use OTel SDKs while still benefiting from Datadog's APM features.

prompt: In APM, what does the error rate metric measure?

choices.text: The percentage of spans that took longer than 1 second
The proportion of requests that resulted in an error out of all requests
The number of times the service crashed and restarted
The ratio of 4xx to 5xx HTTP errors

explanation: Error rate is the proportion of requests (entry spans) that are tagged as errors compared to the total number of requests. A span is marked as an error when the error tag is set to true, typically triggered by an unhandled exception or a non-2xx HTTP status code.

prompt: What is the purpose of 'Unified Service Tagging' in Datadog?

choices.text: To merge metrics, traces, and logs from the same service under consistent env, service, and version tags
To automatically create tags from Kubernetes labels
To synchronize tags between multiple Datadog organizations
To apply a single monitor to all services simultaneously

explanation: Unified Service Tagging standardizes the env, service, and version tags across metrics, traces, and logs from the same service. This enables seamless navigation between signals, for example, pivoting from a trace to related logs or metrics for the exact same deployment version.

prompt: Which of the following are valid span status/error indicators in Datadog APM? (Select all that apply)
explanation: Datadog APM uses the span tags error.type (the exception class), error.message (the error description), and error.stack (the stack trace) to capture error details on a span. error.code is not a standard Datadog APM span tag for errors.

prompt: What is the default port the Datadog Agent listens on for incoming APM trace data?
explanation: The Datadog Agent listens for APM trace data on port 8126 (HTTP/TCP) by default. Port 8125 is used for DogStatsD metrics. Port 4317 is the default OTLP gRPC port. Port 9090 is not a standard Datadog port.

prompt: Trace context propagation in Datadog uses HTTP headers to pass the trace ID and span ID between services in a distributed system.
explanation: Datadog uses HTTP headers (such as x-datadog-trace-id, x-datadog-parent-id, and x-datadog-sampling-priority) to propagate trace context between services. This allows the receiving service to continue the same trace rather than starting a new one.

prompt: Which view in the Datadog APM UI shows a flame graph-style breakdown of time spent in each service and span within a single trace?
explanation: The Trace Detail view (accessible by clicking on an individual trace) displays a waterfall/flame graph of all spans in the trace, showing their parent-child relationships, duration, and time distribution across services. This helps identify bottlenecks and slow spans.

prompt: What is a 'deployment tracking' use case in Datadog APM?

choices.text: Tracking which engineers deployed code changes
Comparing latency, error rate, and throughput between the current version and the previous version of a service
Tracking which AWS regions a service is deployed in

explanation: Deployment Tracking in Datadog uses the version tag (from Unified Service Tagging) to compare the current deployment's APM metrics, latency, error rate, throughput, against the previous version. This helps engineers quickly identify regressions introduced by a new deployment.

prompt: Datadog APM Error Tracking groups errors from both backend traces and frontend Real User Monitoring (RUM) into the same unified view.
explanation: Datadog Error Tracking is a unified product that groups errors from APM traces, logs, and RUM (frontend JavaScript errors) into a single Issues view. This allows teams to see the full picture of errors across the stack in one place.

prompt: What is 'span filtering' or 'ingestion control' in Datadog APM?

choices.text: Blocking specific IP addresses from being traced
Configuring rules to drop certain spans before they are sent to Datadog, reducing ingestion volume
Preventing spans from appearing in the Service Map

explanation: Ingestion controls (span filtering) allow you to configure rules in the Datadog Agent or tracing library to drop spans that match certain criteria (e.g., health check endpoints) before they are sent to Datadog. This reduces ingestion costs while maintaining visibility into meaningful traffic.

prompt: In the Datadog Service Catalog, what information is centralized for each service?

choices.text: Only the service's current error rate
Ownership, documentation links, SLOs, on-call information, and observability signals for each service
Only infrastructure metrics for the hosts running the service

explanation: The Service Catalog is a metadata store that centralizes ownership (team, on-call), documentation (runbooks, repos, dashboards), SLOs, and live observability data (APM metrics) for every service. It helps organizations maintain service hygiene and discoverability.

prompt: Which of the following are features of Datadog APM Trace Search? (Select all that apply)

choices.text: Full-text search over span tags and resource names
Aggregating trace data into analytics facets and top lists
Real-time alerting on trace latency anomalies
Viewing individual trace waterfalls from search results

explanation: Trace Search supports full-text search over indexed span tags, aggregation into faceted analytics, and viewing individual trace waterfalls. Real-time alerting on APM metrics is done through Monitors (not Trace Search itself), and query de-obfuscation is a data scrubbing feature, not a Trace Search feature.

prompt: What is 'distributed context propagation' in APM?

choices.text: Broadcasting metrics to multiple Datadog organizations simultaneously
The mechanism by which trace IDs and sampling decisions are passed between services so all spans from a request share the same trace
Replicating the Datadog Agent configuration across multiple hosts
Propagating alert notifications to multiple team channels

explanation: Distributed context propagation is the process of passing trace context (trace ID, parent span ID, sampling priority) from service to service, typically via HTTP headers or message queue metadata. This ensures all work done on behalf of a single user request is stitched together into one trace.

prompt: In Datadog APM, p95 latency is always higher than or equal to p50 latency for the same dataset.
explanation: By definition, the 95th percentile value is always greater than or equal to the 50th percentile value for the same dataset. p95 represents the latency that 95% of requests fall under, while p50 represents the latency that 50% of requests fall under. Since p95 captures a higher fraction, its value must be at least as large.

prompt: Users click a button in the frontend and complain that the action is slow. In an exam scenario, which Datadog concept explains the full request across the web tier and backend service?

choices.text: A trace made up of spans
A dashboard widget made up of measures
A host map made up of tiles
A log archive made up of indexes

explanation: A trace represents the complete end-to-end path of one request as it moves across services, and spans are the individual timed operations inside that trace. This is the right mental model for app-flow troubleshooting questions that ask where latency was introduced.

prompt: A checkout request touches a web service, a payments API call, and a database query. In an exam scenario, what are those timed individual operations inside the overall request called?
explanation: A trace is the full request path, while each timed operation within that path is a span. Fundamentals questions often test whether you can separate the full request concept from the individual pieces of work inside it.

prompt: After a deployment, one backend service starts failing downstream calls. Which Datadog APM view is best suited to a fundamentals scenario about visualizing service dependencies and unhealthy connections?
explanation: The Service Map is built to show service-to-service dependencies and the health of those connections. That makes it the expected answer when an exam question asks how to visualize which downstream relationships are involved in an application failure.

prompt: A team needs to reduce total trace volume but still keep all traces that contain errors once the full request outcome is known. Which sampling approach best matches this fundamentals scenario?
explanation: Tail-based sampling waits until the trace completes, so it can keep traces based on outcomes like errors or high latency. That is the correct answer when an exam question emphasizes preserving the most valuable traces after the request is complete.
